title: Israeli Airstrikes Kill Three Palestinians in Separate Attacks Despite Ceasefire
detail: Israeli military forces killed three Palestinian men in two separate airstrikes in Gaza on March 30, 2026, continuing targeted operations despite the US-brokered ceasefire that took effect in October 2025. The attacks marked the continuation of a pattern of IDF strikes against individuals designated as militants or Hamas-affiliated security personnel. At least 680 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli fire since the October 2025 ceasefire began, according to Gaza's Government Media Office and Palestinian tracking groups. Israel maintains that the strikes target combatants under a framework consistent with ceasefire provisions that exclude militant activity. The ongoing killings have drawn criticism from UN officials and human rights organizations who say Israel is violating the terms of the ceasefire.

media.caption: Israeli airstrikes kill Palestinians in Gaza in latest ceasefire violations

title: Houthis Launch Ballistic Missile at Israel — First Attack Since Gaza Ceasefire Began
detail: Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen launched a ballistic missile targeting Israel on March 30, 2026, marking the first Houthi attack on Israel since the Gaza ceasefire was announced in October 2025. The missile was intercepted by Israeli air defenses. The attack represented a significant escalation signal from the Iran-aligned bloc, which has publicly stated it holds fire only when Gaza hostilities pause. The resumption of Houthi fire coincides with an intensified US-Israel military campaign against Iran, and with stalled ceasefire Phase 2 negotiations. Israeli officials said air defenses performed as expected. The Houthi attack was described by analysts as a symbolic reminder of the 'Axis of Resistance' solidarity with Hamas, timed to coincide with deteriorating diplomatic progress on Gaza disarmament talks.

media.caption: Yemen's Houthis launch first ballistic missile at Israel since the Iran war began

title: UNRWA Chief Calls for UN Investigation into Killing of 390+ Agency Staff in Gaza War
detail: UNRWA Commissioner-General Philippe Lazzarini on March 30 announced that discussions are underway to establish a formal UN investigation into the killing of more than 390 UNRWA employees during the Gaza war — the deadliest conflict in the history of the United Nations. Lazzarini said the unprecedented death toll, which exceeds any previous UN staff casualty count in any conflict, demands accountability. The 391 confirmed UNRWA staff killed represent a significant portion of the agency's operational personnel in Gaza. Many were killed in strikes that hit UNRWA-marked schools and shelters designated as humanitarian sites. Israel has accused some UNRWA employees of links to Hamas, a claim the agency has said applies to only a small number of individuals. The proposed investigation would be independent, examining whether international humanitarian law was violated in attacks on UNRWA facilities and personnel.

media.caption: Outgoing UNRWA chief calls for investigation into killing of 390+ agency staff in Gaza
